article_title: Thinking Machines Lab Signs Multibillion-Dollar Google Cloud Deal for GB300 Compute, Adding a Second Hyperscaler to Its Stack

content_preview.title: Thinking Machines Lab Signs Multibillion-Dollar Google Cloud Deal for GB300 Compute, Adding a Second Hyperscaler to Its Stack
content_preview.summary: Mira Murati's startup expands its Google Cloud relationship with a non-exclusive multibillion-dollar deal centered on GB300 systems and reinforcement-learning workloads behind its Tinker fine-tuning API.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Thinking Machines Lab, the AI startup founded by former OpenAI chief technology officer Mira Murati, has signed a multibillion-dollar agreement with Google Cloud for compute infrastructure built on Nvidia's GB300 chips, [TechCrunch reported](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/22/exclusive-google-deepens-thinking-machines-lab-ties-with-new-multi-billion-dollar-deal/) on April 22, 2026. The deal, valued in the single-digit billions, is non-exclusive and adds Google as a second hyperscaler...

editor_notes.content_quality: Round-2 rewrite cleanly addresses all 4 round-1 findings. Word count adjusted slightly. Structure intact.
editor_notes.source_verification: Re-verified the 4 specific re-attributions: (1) The 'first cloud deal' contradiction is now explicitly reconciled — article presents BOTH source-0's framing ('first time the lab has struck a deal with a cloud services provider') AND source-1's earlier-arrangement note ('previously struck a smaller, undisclosed Google Cloud arrangement'), then clarifies the new agreement as 'first formal multibillion-dollar cloud-services contract'. (2) 'Las Vegas / Google Cloud Next' venue removed entirely. (3) 'Blackwell-family' descriptor removed; article now refers only to 'Nvidia's GB300 chips' per source. (4) 'Less than a year old' wording verbatim from source-1 in quotes. The other claims verified in round 1 still hold.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All round-1 attribution issues cleanly resolved. The internal contradiction is now handled honestly — readers who follow both source URLs will see that the article acknowledges and reconciles the difference between source-0's and source-1's framing.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Unchanged from round 1 — neutral, professional. The 'What We Don't Know' section now also notes the earlier Google Cloud arrangement's terms are not public, which is good editorial discipline.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Clean round-2 fix. The 'first cloud deal' contradiction was the trickiest issue from round 1 — the bot's solution (acknowledge both source framings explicitly) is the editorially correct way to handle source disagreement rather than sweeping it under the rug. APPROVE — ready to merge.
